conversation.message: I feel like my partner is always putting me down. It's like nothing I do is ever good enough.
I'm really sorry to hear that. It sounds very painful to feel that way. Have you talked to your partner about how their words affect you?
I tried, but they just get defensive and say I'm too sensitive.

I haven't really considered that. I just don’t want to make things worse.

I guess that could work. I just want to feel respected.
You deserve to feel respected. Let's think of some safe ways to approach the conversation, and remember, your feelings are valid.

conversation.message: I feel trapped. My husband controls all our finances and I can’t even buy groceries without asking him.
That sounds really difficult and isolating. It's important to have some financial independence. Have you been able to discuss your feelings with him?
Whenever I try to talk about money, he gets angry and says I don't appreciate what he provides.
It's understandable that you're feeling scared to bring it up. Your feelings are valid. Have you thought about ways to create a small financial plan for yourself?
I don’t know where to start. I just feel so helpless.
Let’s take it step by step. One option could be to set up a savings account in your name, if that's possible. It might help you feel more secure.
That might help. What if he finds out?
If you decide to do this, it’s essential to keep it discreet. Also, consider having a plan for support, like a trusted friend or family member, in case you need to talk or need help.

conversation.message: I've been feeling really trapped lately. My partner controls all our finances and I barely have any money of my own.
I'm really sorry to hear that. It sounds very difficult for you. Can you tell me more about how this affects your daily life?
I can't even buy groceries without asking for money. It makes me feel so powerless and anxious all the time.
That sounds incredibly stressful. It's important for you to have some control over your own finances. Have you thought about any ways to create a safety plan for yourself?
I'm not sure what that would look like. I feel stuck.
Creating a safety plan can help you feel more secure. It might involve setting aside some money secretly, or finding a trusted friend or family member you can talk to about your situation.
I think I might be able to talk to a friend. That could help.
That's a great first step. Remember, you're not alone, and there are resources available to support you. Would you like to explore any local services together?
